name: Skyfield (Astronomical Positions and Ephemeris)

auth.notes: Local library; downloads ephemeris data from NASA JPL (one-time)

performance.notes: NumPy vectorized; fast for batch time computation

agent_readiness.notes: Precise astronomical positions and rise/set times. from skyfield.api import load, wgs84. planets = load('de421.bsp'). earth = planets['earth']. ts = load.timescale(). t = ts.now(). astrometric = earth.at(t).observe(planets['mars']). alt, az, d = astrometric.apparent().altaz(). Sun rise/set: from skyfield.api import N, W. location = earth + wgs84.latlon(40.7128 * N, 74.0060 * W). f = almanac.find_discrete(t0, t1, almanac.sunrise_sunset(planets, location)). Satellite: from skyfield.api import EarthSatellite. TLE loading. ISS tracking.

gotchas: Requires downloading JPL ephemeris files (de421.bsp, ~17MB) on first run
Timescale download may fail without internet — cache locally
Coordinate frame distinctions (GCRS, ICRS, apparent, astrometric) can confuse
No type annotations — poor IDE support
best_when: Precise planetary positions (Mars, Jupiter, etc.) with NASA ephemeris
Sunrise/sunset and moonrise/moonset times for any location
avoid_when: Simple approximate astronomy (use PyEphem for quick estimates)
When ephemeris download is not possible
summary: Skyfield provides precise astronomical positions and ephemeris calculations. load('de421.bsp'). earth.at(t).observe(planets['mars']).apparent().altaz(). Sunrise/sunset via almanac. TLE satellite tracking. MIT by Brandon Rhodes. Better than PyEphem for modern Python. Excellent documentation. Downloads JPL ephemeris (17MB) on first use. Best for precise planet positions, rise/set times, and satellite tracking.
